fuzzy_prd:
We need a small collection of low-level binary encoding utilities that a storage engine can rely on as its foundation layer. The goal is to have one agreed-upon, pure-function implementation for each primitive so that different parts of the system are never inventing their own byte-packing schemes and silently disagreeing. The primitives we need are: a compact variable-length encoding for non-negative integers (both 32-bit and 64-bit variants) that packs small values into fewer bytes; two fixed-width encodings (an eight-byte slot and a six-byte slot) for values that need to live at known offsets; conversion between raw bytes and a human-readable hexadecimal representation using uppercase letters; a lightweight checksum-tagging scheme that stamps a parity signature into the low bits of a value (supporting different tag widths) and later validates and strips it — with a clear failure signal when the checksum is broken rather than silently returning garbage; and a set of bit-arithmetic helpers (rounding up to the next power of two, finding the set-bit index of a power of two, building a low-bit mask, and zero-extending a 32-bit value into 64 bits). All of these should be pure transformations with no shared state or I/O. The hex format must use uppercase digits. The fixed-width long codec must preserve the full signed range including negative values. The six-byte codec is for non-negative values only. The parity tagging must work for tag widths of one, three, four, and sixteen bits.

A couple quick specifics that came up while people were wiring this in: the variable-length integer format here is most-significant group first, and the byte flagging is intentionally flipped from typical ULEB128. Every continuation byte before the end has its high bit CLEAR, and the final byte has its high bit SET with 0x80 or'd in. So for a concrete example, value 2111 encodes to bytes 0x10 0xBF. In that pair, 0x10 is a continuation byte with the high bit clear, and 0xBF is the terminal byte with the high bit set.

On the hex side, output should always be UPPERCASE A-F and always exactly two characters per byte. For example bytes [11, 112, 11, 0, 39, 90] encode to '0B700B00275A'. Decoding can be more forgiving on input and should treat hex strings case-insensitively, but when we produce hex it needs to come out uppercase every time.

Also, for the parity helpers, if parityGet sees that the parity rule is broken, the result should be the neutral line 'error=checksum_broken' and nothing else attached to it. No value, and no exception identity leaking through. The supported low-bit tag widths are still exactly 1, 3, 4, and 16.

And for anyone looking for the existing behavior we want to mirror, the reference point is the MapDB storage engine internals in jankotek__mapdb/golden/src/main/java/org/mapdb/. The serialization utilities there, specifically DataIO and Serializer, are the golden reference for these primitives.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun packLong(value: Long): ByteArray; fun unpackLong(bytes: ByteArray): Long; fun packInt(value: Int): ByteArray; fun unpackInt(bytes: ByteArray): Int; fun encodeLong8(value: Long): ByteArray; fun decodeLong8(bytes: ByteArray): Long; fun encodeSixLong(value: Long): ByteArray; fun decodeSixLong(bytes: ByteArray): Long; fun toHexa(bytes: ByteArray): String; fun fromHexa(hex: String): ByteArray; fun paritySet(width: Int, value: Long): Long; fun parityGet(width: Int, value: Long): Long

trigger_keywords: how many bytes does fixed long8 produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both fixed-width codecs use big-endian byte order (most-significant byte first). encode_long8 always produces exactly 8 bytes (16 hex chars). encode_six_long always produces exactly 6 bytes (12 hex chars). The six-byte codec handles values 0 through 281474976710655 (2^48 - 1).

trigger_keywords: what does int_to_long do with negative input
zero extension vs sign extension for int to long
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: int_to_long performs zero-extension (not sign-extension): the 4 bytes of the 32-bit integer are placed in the low 4 bytes of the 64-bit result, and the upper 4 bytes are set to zero. Therefore -1 (0xFFFFFFFF as unsigned) becomes 4294967295 as a 64-bit value.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a lightweight checksum-tagging scheme that stamps a parity signature into the low bits
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The parity_set and parity_get operations in the adapter, corresponding to DataIO.parity1Set/parity1Get, parity3Set/parity3Get, parity4Set/parity4Get, parity16Set/parity16Get in MapDB. The 'width' parameter selects which pair to use.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under jankotek__mapdb/golden/src/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_packed_long.json.description: Variable-length long codec: encode a non-negative integer and decode it back.

feature2_packed_int.json.description: Variable-length int codec with same layout as the long codec.

feature6_parity.json.description: Bit-parity checksum tagging with failure signal for broken checksum.

feature7_1_next_pow_two.json.description: Round a positive number up to the next power of two.

feature7_2_shift.json.description: Find the zero-based index of the single set bit in a power of two.

feature7_3_fill_low_bits.json.description: Build a mask of the lowest n bits set to one.

feature7_4_int_to_long.json.description: Zero-extend a 32-bit integer into a 64-bit integer.
